title: Go Fundamentals
description: Introduction to the structure, logic, and basics of Golang.

exercises.title: 01. Introduction
exercises.description: Write a Hello World program.

title: Go-Reloaded
description: Challenge set tackling basic string manipulation, parsing, loops, and conditions.

exercises.title: 01. Join With Punctuation
exercises.description:
Write a function that fixes spacing around punctuation for a simple case.
Given a slice of tokens (words + punctuation), remove the space before punctuation marks.

Requirement: Please use a variable named 'result' for the output string, and loop with 'i, token'.

exercises.title: 02. Binary to Decimal
exercises.description:
Write a function that converts a binary string to decimal.

Requirement:
You must use the variable names 'result' and 'err' inside your function.

exercises.title: 03. Hexadecimal to Decimal
exercises.description:
Write a function that converts a hexadecimal string to its decimal integer value.
Use strconv.ParseInt directly; use it, but explain what base and bitSize mean by writing a comment in the file.

Requirement:
You must use the variable names 'result' and 'err' inside your function.

exercises.title: 04. Explain strings.Fields()
exercises.description:
Explain what strings.Fields() does and why it's useful in this project.
Write your explanation in the comments, you must reach the minimum word count.
exercises.boilerplate:
// Explain what strings.Fields() does and why it's useful in this project
// Write your answer here:

exercises.title: 05. Check Punctuation
exercises.description:
Write a function that checks if a string is a punctuation mark from the projects list.

Requirement:
Please use a variable named 'punctuations' for the array, and 'p' in the loop.

exercises.title: 06. Fix Single Quotes
exercises.description:
Write a function that fixes spacing inside single quotes.

Requirement:
Declare the exact variables: 'result', 'match', and 'inner'.

exercises.title: 07. Uppercase Last N
exercises.description:
Write a function that takes a slice of words and applies uppercase to the last N words.

Requirement:
Please use a variable named 'start' to track the starting index.

exercises.title: 08. A or An
exercises.description:
Write a function that determines whether to use "a" or "an" before a given word.

Requirement:
Name variables exactly: 'firstLetter', 'vowelsAndH' and loop using 'v'.

title: ASCII-Art
description: Learn parsing and advanced string manipulation by printing ASCII graphics.

exercises.title: 01. Format Literal Newlines
exercises.description:
Write a function that replaces literal newlines with actual newlines.
The program must handle an input containing `\n`. Find occurrences of the literal characters `\` and `n` and replace them with an actual newline character.

Requirement: Please use a variable named 'formatted' for the output string.

exercises.title: 02. Validate Printable ASCII
exercises.description:
Write a function that validates if a string contains only printable ASCII characters.
Ensure all characters are between ASCII 32 (space) and ASCII 126 (`~`).

Requirement: Name the return variable 'isValid' and loop using '_, char'.

exercises.title: 03. Calculate Character Start Line
exercises.description:
Write a function that calculates the starting line of a character in the banner file.
Each character has a height of 8 lines, and characters are separated by a new line. Calculate the starting index (line number) in the file array for a given ASCII character.

Requirement: Use a variable named 'asciiOffset' to hold the math calculation.

exercises.title: 04. Explain os.ReadFile()
exercises.description:
Explain what os.ReadFile() does, what it returns, and why it is essential for reading the standard.txt banner file while adhering to the allowed standard Go packages rule.

Write your explanation in the comments. You must reach the minimum word count.

exercises.title: 05. Extract 8 Lines for a Character
exercises.description:
Write a function that extracts exactly 8 lines for a specific character.
Given a slice of strings representing the entire banner file and a starting index, extract the 8 lines of the specific character.

Requirement: Declare a slice named 'charLines' and use a standard 'for' loop to append exactly 8 lines.

exercises.title: 06. Check Only Newlines
exercises.description:
Write a function that handles an edge case of only newlines.
If the argument passed is exactly `\n`, the program should just print an empty newline. Write a function to check if the input string consists *only* of newlines.

Requirement: Use a variable named 'isOnlyNewlines' and return it.

exercises.title: 07. Split File Content Into Lines
exercises.description:
Write a function that splits file content into lines based on newline characters.
When reading the banner file, it must be split into an array of strings. Handle both Unix (`
`) and Windows (`
`) line endings.

Requirement: Use 'strings.ReplaceAll' to normalize '
' to '
' before splitting.

exercises.title: 08. Explain strings.Builder
exercises.description:
Explain what strings.Builder does, how it compares to standard string concatenation (using the '+' operator), and why it is highly recommended for building the horizontal ASCII rows.

Write your explanation in the comments. You must reach the minimum word count.

exercises.title: 09. Split Input by Newline
exercises.description:
Write a function that splits the user argument by newlines.
The input string may contain multiple words separated by `\n` (e.g., "Hello\nThere"). Split the string by the newline character to process each word separately.

Requirement: Please use a variable named 'words' for the slice of strings.

exercises.title: 10. Initialize Output Lines
exercises.description:
Write a function that initializes an array of 8 empty strings.
To build the ASCII representation of a word horizontally, you need an array to hold the 8 lines.

Requirement: Name the return variable 'outputLines' and define it with a length of 8.

exercises.title: 11. Append Character Horizontally
exercises.description:
Write a function that appends a character's ASCII lines to the output array.
Given the 8 lines of an ASCII character and the current 8 lines of the output string, append the character's lines horizontally.

Requirement: Loop from 0 to 7 using variable 'i', and append 'charLines[i]' to 'outputLines[i]'.

exercises.title: 12. Explain the Go fs API
exercises.description:
The project instructions state this project will help you learn about the Go file system (fs) API. Explain what this API does and list two common functions used from the `os` package for data manipulation.

Write your explanation in the comments. You must reach the minimum word count.

exercises.title: 13. Print ASCII Word Lines
exercises.description:
Write a function that prints the generated horizontal ASCII lines.
Once a word's 8 lines are generated, they must be printed separated by actual newlines.

Requirement: Iterate through 'outputLines' and use 'fmt.Println'. Return nothing.
exercises.expectedOutput:
row1
row2
row3
row4
row5
row6
row7
row8

exercises.title: 14. Validate Banner File Length
exercises.description:
Write a function to check if the banner file is valid.
A standard banner file requires 855 lines to hold all ASCII characters from 32 to 126 (each takes 9 lines: 8 content + 1 separator).

Requirement: Use a variable named 'lineCount' to store the length of the slice before checking.
exercises.expectedOutput:
isValidBannerLength(855 lines) -> true
isValidBannerLength(100 lines) -> false

exercises.title: 15. Handle Empty String Input
exercises.description:
Write a function that handles empty string inputs.
If the argument passed is an empty string `""`, the program should not print anything.

Requirement: Use a simple 'if' check. Return true if the string is empty, otherwise false.

exercises.title: 16. Explain os.Args
exercises.description:
Explain what `os.Args` is in Go, why `os.Args[0]` is ignored in this project, and how you ensure the program doesn't panic when a user runs `go run .` without passing any arguments.

Write your explanation in the comments. You must reach the minimum word count.

exercises.title: 17. Handle Consecutive Newlines
exercises.description:
When input contains consecutive newlines like `"Hello\n\nThere"`, strings.Split yields an empty string `["Hello", "", "There"]`. Write a function that returns true if a word in the slice is an empty string, indicating an isolated newline should be printed.

Requirement: Name the return variable 'shouldPrintEmptyLine'.

exercises.title: 18. Generate Spaces String
exercises.description:
Write a function that generates a string of spaces based on a multiplier.
Write a function that returns a string consisting of `n` spaces.

Requirement: You must use 'strings.Repeat' and store the result in a variable named 'spaceStr'.

exercises.title: 19. Explain Good Practices
exercises.description:
The project states the code must respect good practices. Name three common Go formatting or structural rules (like using gofmt, descriptive variable names, or proper error handling) that would be evaluated during an audit.

Write your explanation in the comments. You must reach the minimum word count.

exercises.title: 20. Master Process Word Function
exercises.description:
Write a master execution function that ties it all together for one word.
Write a function that takes a word (like "hello"), reads a mock `fileLines` array, and returns the fully assembled `outputLines` array (length 8). Assume `calculateStartLine` and `extractChar` exist.

Requirement: Define the variable 'finalOutput' using 'initOutputLines'. Loop over each rune in the word.
